summary: A brief overview of Friedrich Nietzsche's philosophy of values as described by Alexander V. Razin. The article highlights Nietzsche's call for a re-evaluation of all values, his distinction between master and slave morality, and his emphasis on self-creation and the affirmation of life over conventional, supernatural-based ethics.

principles.statement: Assert your own will and create your own values.
principles.rationale: To achieve self-creation and the affirmation of life, one must avoid being limited by the false and outmoded values of the mediocre masses.
Religious assumptions, such as the immortality of the soul or a personal God acting as a moral judge, are invalid in a rigorous, atheistic inquiry into human values.

mental_models.name: Master vs. Slave Morality
mental_models.description: A lens for analyzing societal values by distinguishing between the 'slave morality' of the masses and the 'master morality' of superior individuals who elevate human society through intellectual creativity.

anti_patterns.description: Adhering to conventional morality and traditional systematic philosophy.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It limits individuals to the false and outmoded values of the mediocre masses and relies on unfounded religious assumptions rather than the affirmation of life.
While Nietzsche rejected conventional morality, he opposed total moral nihilism, instead advocating for a rigorous re-evaluation and creation of new values.
